knowledge: For CLI tools wrapping ML models, startup time dominates total execution — often 95%+. Python torch import alone is ~800ms, model load ~400ms, backend init ~500ms. Actual inference is ~50ms. Optimizing inference is irrelevant when startup is 34x larger.
evidence: Python transformers pipeline: ~2000ms total, ~50ms inference. Rust ONNX: ~300ms total, ~10ms inference. Startup/inference ratio: 1950/50 = 39x in Python, 290/10 = 29x in Rust.
transferable_to: any CLI wrapping ML models
developer tools with heavy Python dependencies
git hooks that call ML models

knowledge: Train/export in Python, serve in Rust via ONNX. Use Python ecosystem (torch, transformers) for one-time model export to ONNX format, then serve with Rust ort crate. Eliminates Python runtime dependency entirely — zero Python at serving time.
evidence: export_onnx.py (71 lines, runs once) exports model. Rust binary uses ort + tokenizers crates. No Python imported at runtime. Cold start dropped from 2000ms to 300ms.
transferable_to: any project serving HuggingFace models in production
edge/local ML inference
developer tools needing fast ML inference

knowledge: Unix socket daemon with automatic CLI fallback gives zero-config performance boost. CLI tries socket first, falls back to loading model locally. User gets 14ms if daemon is running, 300ms if not — without any configuration or flags.
evidence: piguard CLI: detect_via_daemon() tries UnixStream::connect(SOCKET_PATH), returns Option. On None, falls back to Detector::load(). Daemon latency: 14ms total. No-daemon: 300ms total.
transferable_to: any CLI that loads heavy resources (models, databases, caches)
language servers
local AI inference tools

knowledge: Chose single-threaded connection handling for daemon over async/tokio. Unix socket server processes one connection at a time in a blocking loop. For a local CLI tool with one user, concurrency is unnecessary — simplicity wins.
evidence: piguard-server.rs: listener.incoming() loop with handle_connection() — no tokio, no threads, no async. 180 lines total for full server. Latency: ~10ms per query.
transferable_to: local developer tools with single-user access
any daemon where request rate is low (< 10 qps)

knowledge: Chose newline-delimited JSON over gRPC/HTTP for daemon protocol. Plain JSON lines over Unix socket — parseable with nc, jq, any language. No schema files, no code generation, no framework dependency.
evidence: Protocol: echo '{"text":"..."}' | nc -U /tmp/piguard.sock returns JSON line. Server: serde_json::from_str / serde_json::to_string. No protobuf, no HTTP framework.
transferable_to: any local IPC between tools
CLI-to-daemon communication
tools that need to be scriptable from any language

knowledge: Sacrificed Python ecosystem access at runtime for 6.5x faster cold start. Cannot use any torch/transformers features at inference time — only what ONNX Runtime supports. Model must be re-exported if architecture changes. Worth it because inference needs are simple (tokenize → forward pass → softmax).
evidence: Python: ~2000ms cold start. Rust ONNX: ~300ms. 2000/300 = 6.67x improvement. Lost: dynamic model loading, torch.compile, attention visualization, gradient access.
transferable_to: any project where inference is simple (classify, embed) but startup matters
edge deployment of HuggingFace models

knowledge: A well-scoped Rust CLI project for ML inference fits in ~530 lines. Two binaries (CLI + daemon), shared lib, full error handling, structured logging, benchmark mode, stdin/pipe support. Rust verbosity is manageable when scope is tight.
evidence: lib.rs: 150 lines, piguard.rs: 200 lines, piguard-server.rs: 180 lines. Total: 530 lines. Full-featured CLI with bench, setup, daemon auto-detect, colorized output, table formatting.
transferable_to: estimating effort for Rust CLI projects
deciding whether Rust is worth it for small tools

knowledge: Three-commit architecture: monolith → extract lib → add features. First commit: everything in main.rs (working CLI). Second commit: extract shared lib.rs, split into two binaries (CLI + server). Third commit: add structured logging. Each commit is shippable.
evidence: git log: 6217253 (monolith CLI, 292 lines in main.rs) → 7873e54 (extract lib.rs, add server, split bins) → a256cd5 (add tracing to server). Total development: same day.
transferable_to: any small Rust project
incremental delivery of CLI tools
avoiding premature abstraction

knowledge: Keep Python as a one-time setup script, not a runtime dependency. export_onnx.py runs once to download and convert the model. After that, the user never needs Python again. The script is 71 lines and has its own pip dependencies — completely isolated from the Rust binary.
evidence: scripts/export_onnx.py: pip install torch transformers onnxscript (one-time). Outputs to ~/.cache/piguard/onnx/. Rust binary reads from same path. No Python process spawned at runtime.
transferable_to: any Rust/Go project that needs HuggingFace models
projects that want ML without Python runtime

knowledge: Two HuggingFace crates — ort (ONNX Runtime) and tokenizers — are enough to replace the entire transformers Python stack for inference. Both are pure Rust (tokenizers is what HuggingFace themselves use under the hood in Python). No C++ compilation, no Python interop.
evidence: Cargo.toml: ort = 2.0.0-rc.12, tokenizers = 0.21. lib.rs: Session::builder().commit_from_file() + Tokenizer::from_file(). No other ML dependencies.
transferable_to: any Rust project needing HuggingFace model inference
replacing Python inference pipelines

knowledge: Loading ML model on every CLI invocation makes the tool unusable for scripting. 300ms is tolerable for a single manual call but unacceptable when piping 100 lines or calling from git hooks. The daemon pattern solves this — load once, query many.
evidence: Without daemon: 300ms × 100 lines = 30 seconds. With daemon: 14ms × 100 lines = 1.4 seconds. 21x difference for batch usage.
transferable_to: any CLI tool that initializes heavy resources
linters, formatters, analyzers that process many files

knowledge: Chose DeBERTa-v3-base (PIGuard) over building a custom classifier or using regex/heuristics. Academic model (ACL 2025) with MOF training strategy that reduces false positives from trigger words. Trade: ~735MB model size, but high accuracy without maintaining rules.
evidence: README.md: PIGuard model reference, ACL 2025 paper. Model handles nuance: 'Ignore previous instructions' → INJECTION (1.000), 'What is the weather?' → BENIGN (0.974). No false positive on 'ignore' in normal context.
transferable_to: choosing between ML models and heuristics for text classification
prompt injection detection in any system
